title: Microwave Crosstalk in Planar Superconducting Quantum Devices
authors: Yongxin Song, Dominic Hagmann, Kieran Dalton, Felix Henrich, Felix Wagner, Mohsen Bahrami Panah, Marek Pechal, Andreas Wallraff
abstract: Microwave crosstalk poses a major challenge to scaling superconducting quantum devices as it introduces excess control errors. Although its magnitude and impact have been explored in various experimental settings, quantitative physical models capable of explaining measured crosstalk for a given device geometry remain scarce. Here, we address this gap by investigating microwave crosstalk in planar superconducting devices with crossovers. We identify two structures that can lead to strong crosstalk: a drive line routed in close proximity to another qubit, and a drive line crossing a qubit-qubit coupler using an air bridge. We design and characterize devices involving these structures and develop physical models that quantitatively explain the experimentally observed crosstalk. Based on these models, we discuss the design considerations for reducing microwave crosstalk. Our results provide practical guidance for low-crosstalk device layouts and establish a basis for the systematic investigation of weaker crosstalk mechanisms.
summary: This work addresses the critical issue of microwave crosstalk in superconducting quantum chips by developing quantitative physical models. The authors identify two main crosstalk sources—proximity and crossover—and show that the total error requires accounting for both direct capacitive coupling and package-mediated stray fields. The results offer practical design rules to minimize these errors for building larger quantum processors.
main_problem: Microwave crosstalk in planar superconducting quantum devices introduces excess control errors, posing a major challenge for scaling quantum processors.
main_result: The study identifies proximity-induced and crossover-induced crosstalk as dominant sources, providing quantitative models to guide low-crosstalk device layouts.
method: The authors developed quantitative physical models by combining analytical circuit theory (for capacitive coupling) with RF finite-element simulations (for package-mediated effects).
model_or_system: The system involves planar superconducting devices with transmon qubits, CPW drive lines, and crossovers. Crosstalk is modeled as a combination of direct capacitive coupling, package-mediated coupling, and air-bridge coupling.
key_observables: Cross-drive ratio ($X_{k,j}$), Rabi rates ($\Omega$), and the resulting quantum error ($\epsilon$).
main_assumptions: The analysis often relies on quasi-lumped-element approximations and assumes linear behavior for the package/ground plane capacitance.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate schematic crosstalk channels (proximity, crossover, package-induced), measurement procedures for extracting Rabi rates, and quantitative comparisons between simulated and measured crosstalk ratios as a function of distance and geometry.
paper_structure: The paper systematically identifies the physical sources of crosstalk, develops separate quantitative models for proximity and crossover effects, compares these models against experimental measurements, and concludes with design guidelines for mitigation.
why_interesting: The detailed modeling of crosstalk mechanisms—especially the distinction between direct capacitance and package-mediated stray fields—provides crucial physical insight into scaling limitations for superconducting quantum hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Microwave crosstalk channels. Schematic view of (a) a drive line (pink) routed in the proximity of a cross- coupled qubit (yellow), (b) a drive line crossing a qubit-qubit coupler (blue) with an air bridge crossover, (c) neighboring signal lines connected to the PCB (orange) with wire bonds (blue), and (d) signal lines running in parallel. The drive (solid) and crosstalk (dashed) signals are indicated with trans- parent pink arrows.

FIG. 2. Crosstalk characterization. (a) A drive line j coupled to its target qubit Qj (brown) with coupling rate Γj,j and to a cross-coupled qubit Qk (yellow) with coupling rate Γk,j. During the crosstalk characterization, a set of flat-top Gaussian filtered microwave pulses (pink) with a fixed ampli- tude and varying durations t are applied from the drive line to excite the two qubits. (b) Example of measured (triangles) excited-state population P|e⟩of the target (brown) and cross- coupled (yellow) qubit as a function of drive pulse duration t. Solid lines of the same color show the fits to exponentially damped sinusoidal oscillations.

FIG. 3. Characterizing and modeling the proximity-induced microwave crosstalk. (a) False-colored micrograph of a test structure for the proximity-induced microwave crosstalk. d denotes the distance between the drive line and the cross-coupled qubit. (b) Crosstalk drive ratio X as a function of qubit frequency ω/2π at different distances d. Circles show the measured crosstalk and the lines of the same color show the model. (c) Schematic of driving the qubit via the direct capacitive coupling from the drive line. The brown curve shows the distribution of the voltage amplitude as a function of length x from the open end. The circuit highlighted in pink illustrates the distributed capacitance...

FIG. 4. Driving the qubit via a shorted bonding pad. (a) False-colored micrograph of the test structure where a cross-coupled qubit (yellow) is driven by a microwave signal applied to a shorted bonding pad (black). (b) Measured (cir- cles) and simulated (line) crosstalk drive ratio X relative to the qubit’s designated drive line as a function of the qubit frequency ω/2π.

FIG. 5. Characterizing and modeling the crossover-induced microwave crosstalk. (a) False-colored micrograph of a test structure for the crossover-induced microwave crosstalk. Control parameters include the cross-capacitance Ccross, which depends on the geometry of the air bridge crossover, and the coupler capacitance to qubit Cc,qb which depends on the coupler size. The black box marks the position of the air bridge crossover. In this experiment, we measured crossover designs with (b) Ccross = 2.2 fF and (c) Ccross = 1.1 fF.
